enrichment.short_description: Outgrow is an interactive content marketing platform for building quizzes, calculators, assessments, polls, chatbots, and product recommendations that capture and qualify leads on a website.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (paid plans from $22/month billed monthly, or about $14/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Outgrow Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Outgrow lets marketers build quizzes, calculators, and chatbots for lead generation. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Outgrow is a no-code interactive content marketing platform used by marketers, agencies, and small businesses to create quizzes, calculators, assessments, surveys, polls, contests, and lead-generating chatbots that can be embedded on websites or shared as standalone links. The company was co-founded by Randy Rayess and Pratham Mittal and is headquartered in New York, NY. Outgrow's core value proposition is turning passive website visitors into qualified leads by having them actively engage with a personalized experience, such as a which-product-is-right-for-you quiz or an ROI calculator, with the results used to score and segment leads before they are pushed into a CRM or email marketing tool via native or Zapier-based integrations. The platform includes a drag-and-drop content builder, conditional logic and scoring engines for building branching quizzes and calculators, customizable templates and layouts, A/B testing, analytics dashboards, and white-label branding on higher tiers. Outgrow uses a freemium subscription model: a limited free plan supports basic forms and surveys, while paid plans (Freelancer, Essentials, and Business, plus custom Startup and Enterprise tiers) unlock more content types, higher annual lead caps, additional users, advanced logic, custom domains, and priority support, and annual billing offers a substantial discount over monthly billing. Outgrow primarily targets marketing teams, growth marketers, agencies, and B2B SaaS companies that want to generate leads through engagement rather than static landing pages, and it reports powering content used across 150-plus countries. Its main competitors in the interactive-content and quiz and calculator-builder space include Typeform, Involve.me, ConvertFlow, and Jotform, with Outgrow differentiating itself through its emphasis on lead scoring, calculator-style logic built from formulas and variables, and native integrations aimed specifically at demand generation and marketing teams rather than general-purpose form building. Because every quiz, calculator, or chatbot doubles as a data-capture form, marketing teams commonly use Outgrow to replace or supplement static gated content like whitepapers and pricing pages, betting that an interactive experience will produce higher engagement and a richer data profile on each lead than a traditional form fill. The platform's analytics layer tracks completion rates, drop-off points, and answer-level detail, which agencies in particular use to demonstrate campaign performance to clients across multiple accounts managed from one dashboard. Outgrow's freemium structure, with a genuinely usable entry-level free plan alongside clearly tiered paid plans, makes it approachable for solo marketers and small businesses while still offering the volume, seats, and branding controls that larger marketing teams and agencies need at the Business and Enterprise tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Outgrow is a marketing platform built around one idea: people engage more with content that responds to them. Instead of a static blog post or landing page, Outgrow lets teams build quizzes, calculators, assessments, polls, contests, and chatbots that ask visitors questions and return a personalized result, such as a product recommendation, a savings estimate, or a qualification score.

The platform is aimed squarely at marketing and growth teams rather than developers. Content is built visually with pre-made templates, and each piece of interactive content doubles as a lead capture form, so the data collected can be synced into a CRM, ad platform, or email tool to power follow-up campaigns and segmentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Outgrow is a logic and formula engine that lets non-technical users build branching quizzes and multi-variable calculators without writing code, along with a library of industry-specific templates to speed up setup.

Beyond the builder, Outgrow includes lead scoring rules that assign point values to answers, analytics on completion rates and drop-off points, A/B testing of variants, and native integrations with tools such as HubSpot, Mailchimp, Marketo, and Zapier so qualified leads flow directly into existing marketing and sales workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Outgrow follows a freemium, tiered-subscription model. A free plan is limited to basic forms and surveys with a capped number of leads per year, while paid Freelancer, Essentials, and Business plans progressively raise the number of supported content types, annual lead volume, seats, and available layouts, and add features like custom domain hosting and priority support.

Annual billing is discounted heavily versus monthly billing on every paid tier, and Outgrow also offers custom Startup plans and an Enterprise and agency tier with negotiated pricing for larger teams that need higher volume or more customization.

features.title: Quiz and calculator builder
features.description: Drag-and-drop editor for building branching quizzes, assessments, and multi-variable calculators without code.

features.title: Lead scoring
features.description: Assigns point values to user answers so leads can be automatically qualified and segmented.

features.title: Conditional logic
features.description: Shows or hides questions and results dynamically based on prior answers.

features.title: Templates and layouts
features.description: Library of pre-built industry templates and layout styles to speed up content creation.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Tests variations of interactive content to optimize completion and conversion rates.

features.title: Native integrations
features.description: Connects to CRM, email marketing, and ad platforms, plus Zapier for broader automation.

features.title: Analytics dashboard
features.description: Tracks views, completion rates, and drop-off points for every piece of content.

features.title: White-label branding
features.description: Removes Outgrow branding and applies custom domains and colors on higher-tier plans.

pros: No-code builder makes it accessible to marketers without developer support
Strong lead scoring and qualification features tailored to demand generation
Wide range of content types beyond quizzes, including calculators and chatbots
Free plan available for testing basic forms and surveys before committing to a paid tier
Large template library across many industries
cons: Pricing rises quickly once lead volume or content-type needs increase
Interface and logic builder can feel complex for first-time users
Design customization is more limited than dedicated form or landing page tools
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Some advanced features are gated behind higher tiers even for small teams

faqs.question: What is Outgrow used for?
faqs.answer: Outgrow is used to build interactive content, such as quizzes, calculators, assessments, and chatbots, that capture and qualify leads by engaging website visitors instead of relying on static forms.

faqs.question: Does Outgrow have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outgrow offers a free plan limited to basic forms and surveys with a capped number of leads per year; more advanced content types require a paid plan.

faqs.question: How much does Outgrow cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start around $22 per month billed monthly (about $14 per month billed annually) and scale up through Freelancer, Essentials, and Business tiers, with custom Enterprise pricing for larger teams.

faqs.question: Does Outgrow integrate with my CRM?
faqs.answer: Outgrow offers native integrations with popular marketing and CRM tools such as HubSpot, Mailchimp, and Marketo, plus a Zapier connection for broader automation.

faqs.question: Who is Outgrow best suited for?
faqs.answer: Outgrow is best suited for marketing teams, growth marketers, agencies, and B2B companies that want to generate and qualify leads through interactive content rather than static landing pages.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Outgrow?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Typeform, Involve.me, ConvertFlow, and Jotform, though Outgrow differentiates itself with lead scoring and calculator-style logic aimed at demand generation.

faqs.question: Can Outgrow content be embedded on any website?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outgrow content can be embedded on most websites via code snippet, or shared as a standalone hosted link.

faqs.question: Does Outgrow support A/B testing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outgrow lets users create and test variations of their interactive content to compare completion and conversion rates.

use_cases.title: Lead generation quizzes

use_cases.title: ROI and savings calculators
use_cases.description: Sales and marketing teams create interactive calculators that estimate cost savings or ROI to qualify prospects earlier in the funnel.

use_cases.title: Agency client campaigns
use_cases.description: Marketing agencies build and manage interactive content campaigns across multiple client accounts from one platform.

features: Built for mid-market, enterprise, and agency needs

enrichment.short_description: Outline is a fast, collaborative team knowledge base and wiki for internal documentation, available as a hosted cloud service or self-hosted from its open-source codebase.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, open source) or $10/month for the hosted Starter cloud plan
enrichment.seo_title: Outline Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Outline is an open-source team wiki and knowledge base. Compare 2026 pricing, features, self-hosting options, and alternatives like Notion and Confluence.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Outline is a team knowledge base and wiki application built for internal documentation, product specs, meeting notes, onboarding guides, and support answers. The commercial company behind it was founded at the start of 2020 in New York City, building on roughly three years of prior open-source development, and it describes itself as bootstrapped and profitable rather than venture-funded. Outline is distributed under a source-available open-source license on GitHub, so teams can self-host it for free on their own infrastructure, or use the official hosted cloud version run by the company. Its core differentiator versus general-purpose workspace tools like Notion is speed and a document-first, markdown-native editing experience: a real-time collaborative editor, instant full-text search, nested document collections, revision history, and a command-driven interface aimed specifically at engineering and product-minded teams rather than general note-taking. Outline supports single sign-on, granular team and group permissions, an API and webhooks, and 20-plus integrations including Slack, GitHub, and Figma embeds, plus AI-assisted question answering and document translation on its hosted plans. Pricing for the hosted cloud product is tiered by team size and feature set: a Starter plan for small teams (roughly $10 per month), a Team plan for growing teams (roughly $79 per month) that adds SSO and deeper integrations, a Business plan for larger organizations (roughly $249 per month) that adds API access, audit logs, and email support, and a custom Enterprise tier for teams larger than 200 users; every cloud plan includes a free trial, and self-hosting the open-source version remains free indefinitely aside from infrastructure costs. Outline's target users are software teams, startups, and product organizations that want a fast, developer-friendly internal wiki without the overhead of a heavier enterprise content platform. Its principal competitors include Notion, Confluence, GitBook, and Slab, with Outline most often chosen by technical teams that value markdown compatibility, self-hosting control, and a lightweight, keyboard-driven interface over the broader all-in-one workspace features of tools like Notion.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Outline is built specifically as a team knowledge base: a place for internal documentation, engineering runbooks, product specs, onboarding material, and meeting notes to live in organized, searchable collections rather than scattered across chat and shared drives.

The product exists in two forms. Teams can use the official hosted cloud service, or they can self-host the open-source codebase on their own infrastructure for full control over data residency and cost, since the vast majority of Outline's code, including its editor and website, is publicly available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Documents in Outline are organized into nested collections, edited in a real-time collaborative markdown-based editor with commenting and @mentions, and tracked with full version history so changes can always be reviewed or reverted.

On top of the core editing experience, Outline adds instant full-text search, single sign-on and group-based permissions for larger teams, an API and webhooks for building custom workflows, and AI features such as question answering over existing documentation and multi-language translation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The self-hosted, open-source version of Outline is free to run on a team's own servers, with the only ongoing cost being infrastructure. For teams that prefer not to manage hosting, Outline offers a cloud service billed by team size across Starter, Team, and Business tiers, each unlocking more integrations, permission controls, and support as team size grows.

All cloud plans include a 30-day free trial, and nonprofit or educational organizations can qualify for a discount. Organizations with more than 200 users are directed to a custom Enterprise plan negotiated directly with Outline's sales team.

features.title: Real-time collaborative editor
features.description: Markdown-based document editor supporting simultaneous multi-user editing and inline comments.

features.title: Nested collections
features.description: Organizes documents into hierarchical collections so knowledge bases stay structured as they grow.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Instant search across all documents, titles, and content within a workspace.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Tracks every document revision so past versions can be reviewed or restored.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Open-source codebase can be deployed on a team's own infrastructure for free.

features.title: SSO and permissions
features.description: Single sign-on plus group- and role-based access controls on Team and Business plans.

features.title: API and webhooks
features.description: Programmatic access to documents and events for building custom integrations.

features.title: AI question answering and translation
features.description: AI-assisted answers sourced from existing documentation, plus multi-language document translation.

pros: Open-source codebase allows free, fully controlled self-hosting
Fast, keyboard-driven editor built specifically for engineering and product teams
Markdown compatibility makes content portable and developer-friendly
Bootstrapped, profitable company with a long track record of stability
Clean, focused feature set compared to broader all-in-one workspace tools
cons: Fewer all-in-one features (databases, project management) than tools like Notion
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Cloud pricing is per-plan-tier based on team size, which can jump significantly as headcount grows
Smaller integration ecosystem than larger enterprise wiki platforms
Advanced permission and audit features are reserved for higher-priced plans

faqs.question: Is Outline open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the majority of Outline's codebase, including its editor and website, is open source and available on GitHub, allowing teams to self-host it for free.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Outline for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting the open-source version is free aside from your own infrastructure costs; the hosted cloud version is a separate paid service.

faqs.question: How much does Outline's hosted plan cost?
faqs.answer: Outline's cloud plans start at about $10 per month for the Starter tier, with Team and Business tiers priced higher based on team size and features.

faqs.question: What is Outline best used for?
faqs.answer: Outline is best used as an internal team knowledge base for documentation, product specs, meeting notes, and onboarding content, particularly for engineering and product teams.

faqs.question: Does Outline support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSO authentication is available starting on the Team plan.

faqs.question: How is Outline different from Notion?
faqs.answer: Outline is more narrowly focused on documentation and wikis, with a faster markdown-native editor and a self-hostable open-source option, whereas Notion is a broader all-in-one workspace with databases and project tools.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Outline's cloud plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, all cloud plans include a 30-day free trial.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Outline?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Notion, Confluence, GitBook, and Slab.

use_cases.title: Engineering documentation
use_cases.description: Engineering teams maintain runbooks, architecture docs, and onboarding guides in searchable, version-controlled collections.

use_cases.title: Company-wide internal wiki
use_cases.description: Organizations centralize policies, meeting notes, and cross-team knowledge in a single searchable workspace.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted knowledge base for data-sensitive teams
use_cases.description: Teams with strict data residency or compliance requirements self-host Outline on their own infrastructure instead of using a third-party cloud service.

enrichment.short_description: Outplay is a multichannel sales engagement and outreach automation platform that helps sales teams run email, calling, LinkedIn, and WhatsApp sequences from one workspace.
enrichment.seo_title: Outplay Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Outplay is a multichannel sales engagement platform for outreach and calling sequences. See 2026 pricing, features, pros, cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Outplay is a sales engagement and outreach automation platform that lets sales teams build and run multichannel sequences across email, phone, LinkedIn, SMS, and WhatsApp from a single workspace, combined with a built-in dialer, conversation intelligence, and CRM integrations. It was founded in 2019 by brothers Laxman Papineni and Ram Papineni and has operated out of San Francisco, California. In March 2025, Outplay was acquired by JungleWorks, a company known for its no-code commerce and delivery software stack, which committed to invest further in Outplay while the Papineni brothers continued to lead day-to-day operations; the acquisition was aimed at accelerating investment in AI-driven sales tools such as AI SDR agents. Outplay's product spans prospecting and sequence automation, an AI-assisted email and sequence writer, a dialer with call recording and transcription, website visitor tracking, a Chrome extension for prospecting from LinkedIn and other sites, and native integrations with CRMs including Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics 365. The platform uses a freemium pricing model: a Free Forever plan supports a limited number of active prospects and basic multichannel outreach, while paid Starter, Growth, and Enterprise plans, billed annually per user, progressively add more mailboxes, unlimited prospects and sequences, advanced dialer features, SSO, and a dedicated customer success manager; additional power-ups such as email warm-up, AI SDR agents, and extra calling minutes are sold as add-ons. Outplay primarily targets small and mid-market B2B sales teams and sales development representatives that need an affordable, all-in-one alternative to enterprise sales engagement suites. Its main competitors include Outreach, Salesloft, Apollo.io, and Klenty, with Outplay differentiating itself on price, an all-in-one free tier, and a broad channel mix (including WhatsApp) aimed at growing sales teams rather than large enterprise deployments. Outplay markets itself heavily on affordability relative to enterprise incumbents, packaging a built-in dialer, conversation recording and transcription, website visitor identification, and a Chrome extension for LinkedIn prospecting into its core plans rather than charging separately for each capability the way some larger competitors do. Optional power-ups, such as email deliverability warm-up, additional calling minutes, extra phone numbers, and AI SDR functionality, let growing teams add capacity incrementally instead of jumping to a much more expensive tier. Following the JungleWorks acquisition, Outplay has signaled continued investment in AI-driven prospecting and an expanded CRM offering, positioning the company to compete more directly with larger sales engagement vendors while retaining its lower-cost, SMB-and-mid-market-friendly pricing structure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Outplay is a sales engagement platform designed to let sales reps run coordinated outreach across multiple channels, including email, phone calls, LinkedIn, SMS, and WhatsApp, from a single sequence rather than juggling separate tools for each channel.

The platform combines sequence automation with a built-in dialer, conversation recording and transcription, and website visitor tracking, aiming to give small and mid-market sales teams many of the same capabilities as larger, more expensive sales engagement suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core to Outplay is its multichannel sequence builder, which lets reps combine email, calls, LinkedIn touches, SMS, and WhatsApp steps into a single automated cadence, supported by an AI sequence and email writer and a library of pre-built templates.

Beyond sequencing, Outplay includes a power dialer with automatic voicemail drop and call recording, conversation intelligence with transcription, a Chrome extension for prospecting, and native CRM integrations with platforms like Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics 365, plus Slack and SSO on higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Outplay offers a Free Forever plan with a capped number of active prospects and basic multichannel features, making it accessible to very small teams or individual reps at no cost.

Paid plans, billed per user annually, scale from Starter through Growth to Enterprise, each raising mailbox limits, unlocking more channels and CRM integrations, and adding features like advanced reporting, SSO, and a dedicated success manager. Optional add-ons, called power-ups, cover things like email warm-up, additional calling minutes, and AI SDR functionality.

features.description: Identifies and tracks visitors on a company's website for follow-up outreach.

features.title: CRM integrations
features.description: Native two-way sync with Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics 365, and other CRMs.

features.title: Chrome extension
features.description: Prospects and adds contacts directly from LinkedIn and other web pages.

features.title: A/B testing
features.description: Tests different sequence variants to optimize reply and conversion rates.

features.title: Sales analytics and reporting
features.description: Advanced reporting on sequence performance and rep activity on higher-tier plans.

pros: Free Forever plan makes it accessible to individual reps and very small teams
Broad channel coverage including WhatsApp, which is uncommon among competitors
Built-in dialer and conversation intelligence reduce the need for separate tools
Priced lower than many enterprise sales engagement platforms
Backed by continued investment following its 2025 acquisition by JungleWorks
cons: Advanced calling and reporting features require the top Enterprise tier
Many capabilities are gated behind paid add-ons rather than included in base plans
Smaller ecosystem and brand recognition than category leaders like Outreach or Salesloft
Per-user annual pricing can add up quickly for larger sales teams
Recent acquisition introduces some uncertainty around long-term product direction

faqs.question: What is Outplay used for?
faqs.answer: Outplay is used by sales teams to automate multichannel outreach sequences across email, calling, LinkedIn, SMS, and WhatsApp, combined with a built-in dialer and CRM sync.

faqs.question: Does Outplay have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outplay offers a Free Forever plan with a limited number of active prospects and basic multichannel outreach features.

faqs.question: How much does Outplay cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $39 per month for Starter and scale up to $89 and $139 per user per month for Growth and Enterprise, all billed annually with a 7-day free trial.

faqs.question: Who owns Outplay?
faqs.answer: Outplay was acquired by JungleWorks in March 2025, though co-founders Laxman and Ram Papineni continue to lead the company's day-to-day operations.

faqs.question: Does Outplay integrate with Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outplay offers native integration with Salesforce as well as Microsoft Dynamics 365 on its Growth and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Who is Outplay best suited for?
faqs.answer: Outplay is best suited for small and mid-market B2B sales teams looking for an affordable, all-in-one alternative to larger sales engagement platforms.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Outplay?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Outreach, Salesloft, Apollo.io, and Klenty.

faqs.question: Does Outplay support LinkedIn outreach?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outplay includes LinkedIn automation as part of its multichannel sequences, available as a power-up add-on on some plans.

enrichment.short_description: Outreach is an enterprise sales engagement and revenue execution platform used by large sales organizations to automate prospecting, manage deals, forecast pipeline, and coach reps with AI agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Outreach Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Outreach is an enterprise AI sales engagement platform for prospecting, deals, and forecasting. See 2026 pricing, features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Outreach is an enterprise sales engagement platform, now positioned as an agentic AI platform for revenue teams, founded in 2014 by Wes Hather, Gordon Hempton, Manny Medina, Belinda Prince, and Andrew Kinzer and headquartered in Seattle, Washington. Outreach became one of the category-defining companies in sales engagement, raising roughly $489 million across multiple funding rounds from investors including Mayfield, Spark Capital, and Trinity Ventures, and reaching a valuation of about $4.4 billion following a $200 million round; it employs over 1,500 people across offices in Seattle, Atlanta, New York, and San Francisco. The platform, originally built around automated multichannel sales sequences (email, calling, and social touches), has expanded into a broader revenue execution suite covering prospecting and pipeline generation, deal management and risk inspection, AI-assisted sales forecasting, conversation intelligence and rep coaching, and customer retention and expansion workflows. In 2025-2026 the company rebranded its site from outreach.io to outreach.ai and restructured its product around AI agents, including tools such as Outreach Omni (a conversational agent for cross-platform queries and actions), Agent Studio (a visual workflow builder for configuring AI agents), and a Meeting Prep Agent, alongside a consolidated Amplify pricing structure (Amplify Core, Amplify Plus, and Amplify Pro) that layers on features like real-time transcription, deal health scoring, and multi-currency forecasting at higher tiers. Outreach does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote, reflecting its focus on mid-market and enterprise sales organizations rather than small teams. Its core competitors are Salesloft, HubSpot Sales Hub, Apollo.io, and Outplay, with Outreach generally regarded as one of the two dominant, enterprise-grade players (alongside Salesloft) in the sales engagement category, differentiated by its scale, AI agent investment, and depth of forecasting and deal-inspection functionality built for large, complex sales organizations. Outreach's customer base skews toward mid-market and enterprise revenue organizations with dedicated sales operations and revenue operations teams, since the platform's forecasting, coaching, and deal-inspection tools are built to manage complex, multi-stage pipelines across large rep headcounts rather than the simpler needs of a small sales team. The company has continued to expand beyond pure sales engagement into a fuller revenue execution category, adding modules for customer success and renewal risk in addition to its original prospecting and pipeline tools, which further distinguishes it from lighter-weight, prospecting-focused competitors like Outplay and Apollo.io.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Outreach is one of the original category leaders in sales engagement software, built to help sales teams automate outbound prospecting sequences across email, phone, and other channels while giving managers visibility into pipeline and rep performance.

Over its history the platform has grown from a sequence-automation tool into a broader revenue execution suite covering deal management, forecasting, and coaching, and it has more recently repositioned itself around AI agents that can research prospects, flag at-risk deals, and prepare reps for meetings automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Outreach's foundation is multichannel sequence automation combined with an integrated dialer and email tracking, layered with AI agents that handle tasks like prospect research, account stakeholder mapping, and meeting preparation.

On the management side, Outreach provides deal inspection and risk flagging, AI-assisted forecasting and scenario planning, conversation intelligence for coaching reps from call and meeting recordings, and workflow automation tools such as Agent Studio for configuring custom AI-driven processes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Outreach does not publish list pricing. The company sells through a direct sales process, with prospective customers requesting a demo and receiving a custom quote based on team size, modules, and contract terms.

Its current packaging is organized around three tiers, called Amplify Core, Amplify Plus, and Amplify Pro, which progressively add features like real-time call transcription, AI coaching, deal health scoring, and multi-currency forecasting as customers move up the tier structure.

features.title: Multichannel sequences

features.description: Agents such as Outreach Omni and a Meeting Prep Agent automate research, briefings, and account actions.

features.description: Flags at-risk deals and surfaces stakeholder engagement data for active opportunities.

features.title: AI-assisted forecasting
features.description: Generates pipeline and revenue forecasts with scenario planning and multi-currency support on higher tiers.

features.title: Conversation intelligence
features.description: Records and analyzes sales calls and meetings for coaching and buyer insight.

features.title: Agent Studio
features.description: Visual workflow builder for configuring custom AI agent automations.

features.title: Integrated dialer
features.description: Built-in calling with post-call summarization and logging.

features.title: CRM integration
features.description: Two-way sync with Salesforce and other major CRMs to keep pipeline data current.

pros: Deep, enterprise-grade feature set spanning prospecting, deal management, and forecasting
Heavy recent investment in AI agents for research, coaching, and meeting prep
Strong track record and category leadership backed by significant funding and scale
Integrated conversation intelligence reduces the need for a separate coaching tool
Robust reporting and forecasting suited to complex, multi-team sales organizations
cons: Pricing is entirely custom and not disclosed publicly, complicating budget comparisons
Platform complexity and breadth can be overkill for small or simple sales teams
Implementation and onboarding typically require more time than lighter-weight competitors
Cost is generally higher than mid-market alternatives like Outplay or Apollo.io
Recent rebrand and product restructuring may require relearning for existing customers

faqs.question: What is Outreach used for?
faqs.answer: Outreach is used by sales organizations to automate prospecting sequences, manage and inspect deals, forecast revenue, and coach reps using AI-assisted conversation intelligence.

faqs.question: How much does Outreach cost?
faqs.answer: Outreach does not publish pricing publicly; prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote based on their needs and team size.

faqs.question: Is Outreach the same company as Outreach.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, Outreach rebranded its website from outreach.io to outreach.ai around 2025-2026 to reflect its focus on agentic AI, but it remains the same company and product line.

faqs.question: Who founded Outreach?
faqs.answer: Outreach was founded in 2014 by Wes Hather, Gordon Hempton, Manny Medina, Belinda Prince, and Andrew Kinzer.

faqs.question: Where is Outreach headquartered?
faqs.answer: Outreach is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, with additional offices in Atlanta, New York, and San Francisco.

faqs.question: What are Outreach's pricing tiers called?
faqs.answer: Outreach's current packaging is organized into three tiers: Amplify Core, Amplify Plus, and Amplify Pro, each adding more advanced forecasting, coaching, and deal-management features.

faqs.question: Who are Outreach's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Outreach's main competitors include Salesloft, HubSpot Sales Hub, Apollo.io, and Outplay.

faqs.question: Is Outreach suitable for small sales teams?
faqs.answer: Outreach is generally built for mid-market and enterprise sales organizations; smaller teams may find lighter-weight, self-serve platforms more cost-effective.

use_cases.title: Enterprise outbound prospecting
use_cases.description: Large sales development teams run coordinated multichannel outreach sequences at scale with centralized reporting.

use_cases.title: Deal and pipeline management

enrichment.short_description: OVHcloud is a major European cloud infrastructure and hosting provider offering VPS, dedicated servers, public and private cloud, storage, and web hosting from data centers across the world.
enrichment.starting_price: From about $4-6/month for entry-level VPS; dedicated server, public cloud, and enterprise services are billed on usage-based or custom-quoted pricing
enrichment.seo_title: OVHcloud Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OVHcloud is Europe's largest independent cloud provider, offering VPS, dedicated servers, and cloud hosting. See 2026 pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OVHcloud is a European cloud infrastructure and web hosting provider founded in 1999 by Octave Klaba, along with family members, in Roubaix, France, where the company remains headquartered. Starting as a small web hosting business built on borrowed capital, OVH grew rapidly through the 2000s by building and operating its own data centers rather than reselling third-party infrastructure, and it rebranded to OVHcloud in 2019 as it expanded into public cloud, private cloud, and managed services. Today OVHcloud operates dozens of data centers across multiple continents, manages hundreds of thousands of servers, and serves well over a million customers in more than 140 countries, with a workforce of roughly 3,000 to 3,500 employees, making it Europe's largest cloud infrastructure provider independent of the major US hyperscalers (AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure). Its product portfolio spans virtual private servers (VPS), bare-metal dedicated servers, OpenStack-based public cloud compute and storage, private cloud (VMware-based), managed Kubernetes, object and block storage, domain names and web hosting, and network and security services including built-in anti-DDoS protection on most offerings. OVHcloud differentiates itself primarily on price transparency, published self-serve pricing for entry-level infrastructure (VPS plans starting at just a few dollars per month), European data sovereignty and GDPR-aligned hosting, and vertical integration from owning its own data centers, network, and even server manufacturing. Its pricing model blends published monthly subscription rates for standardized products like VPS and dedicated servers with usage-based and custom-quoted pricing for public cloud and enterprise services. OVHcloud's target customers range from individual developers and small businesses buying inexpensive VPS or web hosting up through large enterprises and public sector organizations seeking a European alternative to US-based hyperscalers for data sovereignty or cost reasons. Its main competitors include Hetzner, DigitalOcean, and Scaleway among European infrastructure providers, as well as AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure at the higher end of the market, with OVHcloud generally positioned as a lower-cost, Europe-first alternative to the American hyperscalers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OVHcloud is a French cloud computing and hosting company that has grown from a small regional web host into Europe's largest independent cloud infrastructure provider, built on data centers, networking, and even server hardware that the company designs and operates itself.

The company serves everyone from individual developers renting a low-cost VPS to large enterprises and public sector organizations that choose OVHcloud specifically for European data sovereignty and independence from the major US cloud hyperscalers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: OVHcloud's product line spans virtual private servers, bare-metal dedicated servers, OpenStack-based public cloud, VMware-based private cloud, managed Kubernetes, object and block storage, and domain and web hosting services, all backed by built-in anti-DDoS protection.

Because OVHcloud owns and operates its own data centers and network backbone rather than reselling infrastructure, it is able to publish transparent, relatively low self-serve pricing for standardized products while also offering custom-quoted enterprise and public cloud contracts for larger deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Entry-level products such as VPS plans have clearly published monthly prices, generally ranging from around $4 to $45 per month depending on compute, memory, and storage specifications, with volume and long-term billing discounts available.

Public cloud compute, storage, and managed services are typically billed on a usage or consumption basis similar to other major cloud providers, while large enterprise contracts, private cloud deployments, and dedicated infrastructure at scale are generally quoted individually by OVHcloud's sales team.

features.title: Virtual private servers
features.description: NVMe SSD-backed VPS plans with guaranteed bandwidth and built-in anti-DDoS protection.

features.title: Bare-metal dedicated servers
features.description: Dedicated physical servers with configurable hardware for performance-sensitive workloads.

features.title: Public cloud
features.description: OpenStack-based compute, storage, and managed database services billed on usage.

features.title: Managed Kubernetes
features.description: Fully managed Kubernetes clusters for containerized application deployment.

features.title: Private cloud
features.description: VMware-based private cloud infrastructure for enterprise workloads requiring isolation.

features.title: Object and block storage
features.description: Scalable storage services for backups, archives, and application data.

features.title: Web hosting and domains
features.description: Shared web hosting, email hosting, and domain registration for smaller sites and businesses.

features.title: Anti-DDoS protection
features.description: Network-level DDoS mitigation included by default across most infrastructure products.

pros: Transparent, published pricing for VPS and dedicated servers well below typical hyperscaler rates
European data sovereignty appeals to GDPR-conscious and public sector customers
Vertically integrated infrastructure (own data centers, network, and hardware) supports cost efficiency
Broad global data center footprint despite being a European-first provider
Wide product range covering everything from cheap VPS to enterprise private cloud
cons: Smaller managed-service and marketplace ecosystem compared to AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud
Documentation and support quality can be less polished than the largest US hyperscalers
Historical high-profile data center incidents (including a major fire) have raised reliability concerns for some customers
Enterprise and public cloud pricing requires custom quotes rather than fully transparent self-serve rates
Interface and console are viewed by some users as less refined than competitors like DigitalOcean

faqs.question: What is OVHcloud used for?
faqs.answer: OVHcloud is used to run virtual private servers, dedicated servers, public and private cloud workloads, storage, and web hosting for individuals, businesses, and enterprises.

faqs.question: Where is OVHcloud headquartered?
faqs.answer: OVHcloud is headquartered in Roubaix, France, and was founded there in 1999 by Octave Klaba.

faqs.question: How much does OVHcloud cost?
faqs.answer: Entry-level VPS plans start at roughly $4-6 per month, while dedicated servers, public cloud, and enterprise services are billed on usage-based or custom-quoted pricing.

faqs.question: Is OVHcloud a European alternative to AWS?
faqs.answer: Yes, OVHcloud is widely positioned as Europe's largest independent cloud provider and is often chosen as a European alternative to AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure for data sovereignty and cost reasons.

faqs.question: Does OVHcloud offer managed Kubernetes?
faqs.answer: Yes, OVHcloud offers a fully managed Kubernetes service as part of its public cloud portfolio.

faqs.question: Is DDoS protection included with OVHcloud servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, anti-DDoS protection is included by default on most OVHcloud VPS and dedicated server products.

faqs.question: Who are OVHcloud's main competitors?
faqs.answer: OVHcloud's main competitors include Hetzner, DigitalOcean, and Scaleway, as well as AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Azure.

faqs.question: How many data centers does OVHcloud operate?
faqs.answer: OVHcloud operates dozens of data centers across multiple continents as part of its global infrastructure footprint.

use_cases.title: GDPR-aligned enterprise cloud
use_cases.description: European enterprises and public sector organizations run workloads on OVHcloud for data sovereignty and compliance reasons.

enrichment.short_description: OWASP Dependency-Check is a free, open-source software composition analysis tool that scans project dependencies and flags known publicly disclosed vulnerabilities (CVEs) using CPE matching.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (open-source project maintained by volunteer contributors)
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (community-maintained project under the OWASP Foundation)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OWASP Dependency-Check scans dependencies for known CVEs. Learn how this free, open-source SCA tool works, its features, and top alternatives in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OWASP Dependency-Check is a free, open-source software composition analysis (SCA) tool that scans a project's dependencies and attempts to identify publicly disclosed vulnerabilities within them. Development began around September 2012, making it one of the earliest dedicated SCA tools in the application security space, and it was created and led for many years by Jeremy Long under the OWASP Foundation, the nonprofit best known for the OWASP Top 10 web application security risks list. The tool works primarily by generating evidence about each dependency (such as file hashes, embedded metadata, and package identifiers), mapping that evidence to Common Platform Enumeration (CPE) identifiers, and then cross-referencing those CPEs against the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) to surface associated Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) entries, producing a report of known-vulnerable components with severity scores. It is distributed under the Apache License 2.0 and ships as a command-line tool along with plugins for Maven, Gradle, and Ant, plus integrations for CI/CD systems such as Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps; it also leverages ecosystem-specific vulnerability sources such as the NPM Audit API, OSS Index, RetireJS, and Bundler Audit to extend coverage beyond Java into JavaScript, Ruby, and other languages. Its primary GitHub home moved from the original jeremylong/DependencyCheck repository to a dedicated dependency-check organization to support continued community-driven maintenance. Dependency-Check is used by application security engineers, DevSecOps teams, and individual developers who want a free, self-hosted way to catch known-vulnerable open-source libraries in CI pipelines before they reach production, and it is designated an OWASP Flagship project reflecting its maturity and adoption. Its closest free/open-source alternative is OWASP Dependency-Track, which consumes Dependency-Check-style output for continuous monitoring, while commercial competitors in the broader SCA space include Snyk, Mend (formerly WhiteSource), and GitHub's native Dependabot, with Dependency-Check differentiating itself as a fully free, locally runnable scanner rather than a hosted SaaS platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OWASP Dependency-Check is a software composition analysis tool built to answer a simple but important question: does this project use any open-source libraries with publicly known security vulnerabilities?

Rather than analyzing an application's own source code, Dependency-Check inspects the third-party dependencies pulled in through package managers and build tools, matching them against public vulnerability databases so teams can catch known-vulnerable components before they ship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The tool identifies dependencies and gathers evidence about them, then attempts to map that evidence to Common Platform Enumeration identifiers so it can cross-reference the National Vulnerability Database for matching CVE records, generating a report with CVE IDs, CVSS severity scores, and links to advisories.

It runs as a standalone command-line utility or via build-tool plugins for Maven, Gradle, and Ant, and integrates into CI/CD pipelines including Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps, with additional ecosystem-specific checks pulled from sources like the NPM Audit API, OSS Index, RetireJS, and Bundler Audit.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dependency-Check is entirely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid tiers, license fees, or usage limits since it runs locally or in a team's own CI infrastructure.

Because it is community-maintained rather than sold as a product, there is no vendor support contract available directly through the project; teams that want commercial support and a hosted dashboard experience typically pair it with, or migrate to, tools like OWASP Dependency-Track or a commercial SCA platform.

features.title: CPE-to-CVE matching
features.description: Maps dependency evidence to CPE identifiers and cross-references the NVD to surface known CVEs.

features.title: Multi-ecosystem scanning
features.description: Covers Java and .NET natively, with additional checks for JavaScript, Ruby, and other ecosystems via integrated data sources.

features.title: Build tool plugins
features.description: Available as plugins for Maven, Gradle, and Ant to run scans as part of the normal build process.

features.title: CI/CD integration
features.description: Integrates with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps for automated scanning in pipelines.

features.title: CVSS severity scoring
features.description: Reports include CVSS scores to help teams prioritize which vulnerabilities to remediate first.

features.title: Offline/local scanning
features.description: Runs entirely within a team's own infrastructure without sending code to a third-party service.

features.title: Suppression files
features.description: Lets teams suppress known false positives so they do not repeatedly clutter scan reports.

pros: Completely free and open source with no usage limits
Runs locally or in CI without sending proprietary code to a third party
Long track record as one of the original SCA tools in the OWASP ecosystem
Integrates directly into common build tools like Maven and Gradle
Backed by the well-established NVD vulnerability database
cons: CPE-based matching can produce false positives that require manual suppression
No official hosted dashboard or vulnerability management UI out of the box
Coverage is strongest for Java and .NET, with less mature support for some other ecosystems
No commercial support contract available directly from the project
Requires NVD data feed updates that can be slow or rate-limited without an API key

faqs.question: What is OWASP Dependency-Check used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to scan a project's third-party dependencies and identify any that have publicly disclosed, known vulnerabilities (CVEs).

faqs.question: Is OWASP Dependency-Check free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: How does Dependency-Check identify vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: It gathers evidence about each dependency, maps it to Common Platform Enumeration identifiers, and cross-references the National Vulnerability Database to find matching CVE records.

faqs.question: What languages and ecosystems does it support?
faqs.answer: It has strongest native support for Java and .NET, with additional checks for JavaScript, Ruby, and other ecosystems via integrated data sources like the NPM Audit API and Bundler Audit.

faqs.question: How is Dependency-Check different from OWASP ZAP?
faqs.answer: Dependency-Check is a software composition analysis tool that scans dependencies for known vulnerable libraries, while OWASP ZAP is a dynamic application security testing tool that actively scans and attacks a running web application.

faqs.question: Can Dependency-Check run in a CI/CD pipeline?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, and Azure DevOps, as well as Maven, Gradle, and Ant plugins for build-time scanning.

faqs.question: What are the alternatives to OWASP Dependency-Check?
faqs.answer: Alternatives and complementary tools include OWASP Dependency-Track, Snyk, Mend (formerly WhiteSource), and GitHub Dependabot.

faqs.question: Who maintains OWASP Dependency-Check?
faqs.answer: It was created by Jeremy Long and is maintained by a community of volunteer contributors under the OWASP Foundation.

use_cases.title: CI/CD vulnerability gating
use_cases.description: DevSecOps teams run Dependency-Check as a build step to fail builds when high-severity known-vulnerable dependencies are detected.

use_cases.title: Open-source license and risk audits
use_cases.description: Security teams periodically scan codebases to inventory dependencies and known vulnerabilities for compliance reporting.

use_cases.title: Individual developer dependency checks
use_cases.description: Developers run local scans before committing code to catch known-vulnerable libraries early.

features: Full CLI tool and build plugins

enrichment.short_description: OWASP ZAP (Zed Attack Proxy) is a free, open-source dynamic application security testing tool that acts as an intercepting proxy to actively and passively scan running web applications for vulnerabilities.
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (open-source project; core team is now supported by Checkmarx)
enrichment.seo_title: OWASP ZAP Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: OWASP ZAP is a free, open-source web app security scanner and proxy for penetration testing. See 2026 features, use cases, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: OWASP ZAP, short for Zed Attack Proxy, is a free and open-source dynamic application security testing (DAST) tool used to find security vulnerabilities in running web applications and APIs. It was founded in 2010 by Simon Bennetts as an OWASP project, forked originally from the Paros proxy developed by Chinotec Technologies, and its first public release was announced in September 2010; it grew over the following decade into the most widely used open-source web application security scanner in the world and an OWASP Flagship project. Unlike a software composition analysis tool, ZAP works by sitting as an intercepting proxy between a browser (or automated client) and a target web application, allowing it to passively observe traffic and actively send crafted attack payloads to probe for issues such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and insecure configurations, then report the findings with actionable detail. In 2023, the ZAP project left the OWASP Foundation and moved under the Linux Foundation's Software Security umbrella to secure additional funding and resources, and in 2024 its core team joined the security vendor Checkmarx, with the project rebranded as ZAP by Checkmarx; it remains free, open source under the Apache License 2.0, and community-governed, with Checkmarx providing sponsorship and dedicated engineering resources rather than converting it into a paid product. ZAP's feature set includes an automated active and passive scanner, a traffic-intercepting proxy for manual testing, fuzzing tools, a scripting and automation framework for integrating scans into CI/CD pipelines, and a marketplace of community-built add-ons that extend its capabilities. It is used by penetration testers, application security engineers, and developers who want to run automated or manual DAST scans against staging or test environments without paying for a commercial scanner. Its main alternatives and competitors include Burp Suite (particularly Burp Suite Professional), Acunetix, Invicti (formerly Netsparker), and Nikto, with ZAP differentiating itself as the leading free, fully open-source option in a category where most comparable scanners are commercial products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OWASP ZAP is a dynamic application security testing tool: instead of reading source code or dependency manifests, it actively interacts with a running web application or API to find exploitable vulnerabilities the way an attacker would.

It works as an intercepting proxy, sitting between a browser or test client and the target application, which lets it both passively observe every request and response and actively send crafted, potentially malicious inputs to probe for weaknesses like injection flaws and cross-site scripting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ZAP combines an automated scanner (both passive, which just observes traffic, and active, which sends attack payloads) with manual testing tools such as a traffic interceptor, request replay, and fuzzer, making it useful for both automated pipeline scans and hands-on penetration testing.

An automation framework lets teams script and schedule ZAP scans as part of CI/CD, while a marketplace of community-contributed add-ons extends the core tool with additional scan rules, authentication handlers, and reporting formats.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ZAP is entirely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid tiers or feature gating; every capability, from active scanning to the automation framework, is available at no cost.

Since its core team joined Checkmarx in 2024 as ZAP by Checkmarx, the project has continued as an independently governed open-source effort backed by Checkmarx sponsorship rather than being converted into a commercial product, so pricing and licensing terms for end users have not changed.

features.title: Active and passive scanning
features.description: Automatically probes applications with attack payloads while also passively analyzing observed traffic for issues.

features.title: Intercepting proxy
features.description: Sits between client and server to inspect, modify, and replay web traffic for manual testing.

features.title: Fuzzing
features.description: Sends large sets of crafted inputs to application parameters to uncover edge-case vulnerabilities.

features.title: Automation framework
features.description: Scripts and schedules ZAP scans for integration into CI/CD pipelines.

features.title: Add-on marketplace
features.description: Community-built add-ons extend scan rules, authentication support, and reporting.

features.title: API and application scanning
features.description: Scans traditional web applications as well as REST and SOAP APIs for vulnerabilities.

features.title: Authentication handling
features.description: Supports scanning behind login forms and various authentication schemes.

pros: Completely free and open source with no feature restrictions
Widely regarded as the leading open-source alternative to commercial DAST scanners like Burp Suite
Backed by an active community and, since 2024, Checkmarx sponsorship for continued development
Strong automation framework for embedding scans into CI/CD pipelines
Extensible via a large marketplace of community add-ons
cons: Steeper learning curve than some commercial scanners for advanced manual testing workflows
Active scans can generate false positives that require manual triage
Project governance changes (leaving OWASP, joining Checkmarx) have created some confusion about its status
User interface is less polished than premium commercial alternatives
Scanning complex, heavily JavaScript-driven single-page applications can require additional configuration

faqs.question: What is OWASP ZAP used for?
faqs.answer: OWASP ZAP is used to find security vulnerabilities in running web applications and APIs by actively and passively scanning traffic, similar to how a penetration tester would probe an application.

faqs.question: Is OWASP ZAP free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ZAP is completely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: Is OWASP ZAP still an OWASP project?
faqs.answer: ZAP left the OWASP Foundation in 2023 to join the Linux Foundation's Software Security Project, and in 2024 its core team joined Checkmarx, with the project now branded ZAP by Checkmarx while remaining free and open source.

faqs.question: How is OWASP ZAP different from OWASP Dependency-Check?
faqs.answer: ZAP is a dynamic application security testing tool that actively scans a running web application for vulnerabilities, while Dependency-Check is a software composition analysis tool that scans a project's dependencies for known vulnerable libraries.

faqs.question: Who created OWASP ZAP?
faqs.answer: ZAP was founded in 2010 by Simon Bennetts, forked from the Paros proxy originally developed by Chinotec Technologies.

faqs.question: Can OWASP ZAP be automated in a CI/CD pipeline?
faqs.answer: Yes, ZAP includes an automation framework specifically designed to script and schedule scans as part of CI/CD workflows.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to OWASP ZAP?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Burp Suite, Acunetix, Invicti (formerly Netsparker), and Nikto.

faqs.question: Does OWASP ZAP scan APIs as well as web pages?
faqs.answer: Yes, ZAP can scan REST and SOAP APIs in addition to traditional web applications.

use_cases.title: Automated DAST in CI/CD
use_cases.description: Development teams run ZAP scans automatically against staging environments on every build to catch vulnerabilities before release.

use_cases.title: Manual penetration testing
use_cases.description: Security professionals use ZAP's proxy, fuzzer, and manual testing tools to perform in-depth application penetration tests.

use_cases.title: API security testing
use_cases.description: Teams scan REST and SOAP APIs for injection flaws, authentication weaknesses, and misconfigurations.

enrichment.short_description: Owncast is a free, open-source, self-hosted live video streaming and chat server that lets creators run their own Twitch-style streaming platform on their own infrastructure.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted; server or hosting costs apply)
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (open-source project maintained primarily by its creator and community contributors)
enrichment.seo_title: Owncast Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Owncast is a free, self-hosted live streaming server, an open-source alternative to Twitch. See 2026 features, setup basics, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Owncast is a free and open-source, self-hosted live video streaming and web chat server that lets individuals and communities run their own live streaming platform instead of relying on centralized services like Twitch or YouTube Live. The project was created by developer Gabe Kangas and launched publicly in mid-2020, emerging in part as a response to musicians and independent creators looking for more control over their live streams during the early pandemic period; Kangas remains the primary maintainer alongside a community of open-source contributors. Owncast is built with a Go backend and a React frontend, is distributed under the MIT License, and is designed to be simple to self-host, typically via Docker, on a personal server or a cloud VPS. Once running, broadcasters connect to their own Owncast server using standard streaming software such as OBS Studio, and the server handles video transcoding and delivery to viewers alongside a built-in, customizable web chat with support for custom emotes and chatbots; Owncast also supports federation via ActivityPub, letting streams and chat be discovered and followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms. Because Owncast has no company behind it selling a hosted product, it has no subscription pricing at all: the software itself is entirely free, and the only costs a user incurs are for their own server or VPS hosting and bandwidth. The project sustains its development through donations via Open Collective and sponsorships from companies such as Fastly, Docker, and DigitalOcean, plus merchandise sales, rather than through software licensing revenue. Owncast is aimed at independent streamers, musicians, hobbyists, and privacy- or ownership-minded creators who want full control over their content and community rather than being subject to a platform's algorithm, monetization rules, or moderation policies. Its closest alternatives in the self-hosted and decentralized streaming space include PeerTube (for on-demand and live video) and Livepeer-based tools, while its centralized commercial counterparts are Twitch, YouTube Live, and Kick, with Owncast differentiating itself as a lightweight, easy-to-deploy, fully self-owned alternative rather than a hosted platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Owncast gives individual creators and communities the ability to run their own live streaming service, rather than publishing exclusively through a centralized platform like Twitch. It handles video ingest, transcoding, delivery, and chat, so a single server is enough to run a fully functional streaming site.

The project was created by developer Gabe Kangas and grew out of a desire to give creators, especially musicians during the pandemic, more control and ownership over their live streams and audience relationships than mainstream platforms typically allow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Broadcasters connect to their Owncast server using standard software such as OBS Studio, just as they would with any commercial streaming platform, and Owncast handles transcoding the stream into multiple qualities for viewers.

Alongside video, Owncast includes a built-in, customizable web chat with support for custom emotes and chatbots, an embeddable video player, and ActivityPub-based federation that lets a self-hosted stream be discovered and followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Owncast itself is completely free and open source under the MIT License, with no subscription fees, feature gating, or usage limits imposed by the software.

The only real cost to running Owncast is infrastructure: a server or cloud VPS with enough bandwidth and CPU to handle video transcoding and viewer traffic. The project itself is funded through donations, sponsorships from companies like Fastly and DigitalOcean, and merchandise sales rather than by charging users.

features.title: Self-hosted streaming server
features.description: Runs entirely on infrastructure the user controls, with no dependency on a third-party streaming platform.

features.title: OBS-compatible ingest
features.description: Accepts standard RTMP streams from broadcasting software such as OBS Studio.

features.title: Built-in web chat
features.description: Customizable live chat with support for custom emotes and chatbot integrations.

features.title: ActivityPub federation
features.description: Lets streams and updates be discovered and followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms.

features.title: Video transcoding
features.description: Automatically transcodes incoming streams into multiple quality levels for viewers.

features.title: Embeddable player
features.description: Streams can be embedded on external websites via a standard video player.

features.title: Open-source and self-owned
features.description: Full control over data, moderation, and monetization since there is no third-party platform involved.

pros: Completely free, open-source software with no subscription costs
Full ownership and control over content, chat, and audience data
No platform algorithm, ad policies, or content moderation rules imposed by a third party
ActivityPub support enables discovery through the Fediverse without a centralized platform
Simple Docker-based setup makes self-hosting accessible to non-experts with basic server skills
cons: Requires the user to provide and pay for their own server and bandwidth
No built-in audience discovery comparable to Twitch's or YouTube's recommendation algorithms
Self-hosting means the user is responsible for uptime, scaling, and server security
Smaller ecosystem of extensions and integrations compared to major commercial platforms
No native monetization tools like subscriptions or virtual currency built into the platform

faqs.question: What is Owncast used for?
faqs.answer: Owncast is used to run a self-hosted live video streaming and chat server, giving creators an independent alternative to platforms like Twitch or YouTube Live.

faqs.question: Is Owncast free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast is completely free and open source under the MIT License; the only cost is the server or hosting infrastructure the user runs it on.

faqs.question: Who created Owncast?
faqs.answer: Owncast was created by developer Gabe Kangas, who remains its primary maintainer alongside community contributors.

faqs.question: What streaming software works with Owncast?
faqs.answer: Owncast accepts standard RTMP streams, so it works with common broadcasting software such as OBS Studio.

faqs.question: Does Owncast support chat?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast includes a built-in, customizable web chat with support for custom emotes and chatbot integrations.

faqs.question: Can Owncast streams be found on Mastodon?
faqs.answer: Yes, Owncast supports ActivityPub federation, allowing streams and updates to be followed from Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms.

faqs.question: What are the alternatives to Owncast?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include PeerTube for self-hosted video, as well as centralized commercial platforms like Twitch, YouTube Live, and Kick.

faqs.question: How is Owncast funded if it is free?
faqs.answer: The project is funded through donations via Open Collective, sponsorships from companies like Fastly and DigitalOcean, and merchandise sales.

use_cases.title: Independent musician live streams
use_cases.description: Musicians and performers run their own branded live streaming site instead of depending on a mainstream platform's rules and algorithm.

use_cases.title: Community and hobbyist streaming
use_cases.description: Hobbyist creators and small communities self-host a private or public streaming server for events, gaming, or shows.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused organizational streaming
use_cases.description: Organizations that need full control over stream data and moderation self-host Owncast instead of using a third-party platform.
